rules.sourceTitle: Contaminants | CODEXALIMENTARIUS FAO-WHO
rules.notes: Codex establishes maximum levels (MLs) and guideline levels for contaminants in food and feed. These levels are the maximum concentration legally permitted.
rules.evidenceQuote: The Codex maximum level (ML) for a contaminant in a food or feed commodity is the maximum concentration of that substance recommended by the Codex Alimentarius Commission to be legally permitted in that commodity.

rules.evidenceQuote: This database can serve as a tool to inform about the food additives approved for use in food in the EU and their conditions of use. It is based on the Union list of food additives. This list is in Annex II of Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008.

rules.parameter: Foodborne bacteria (e.g., Salmonella, Listeria monocytogenes, Enterobacter sakazakii), their toxins and metabolites (e.g., staphylococcal enterotoxins, histamine)

rules.notes: Commission Regulation (EC) No 2073/2005 lays down food safety criteria for relevant foodborne bacteria, their toxins and metabolites in specific foods, and process hygiene criteria.
rules.evidenceQuote: Commission Regulation (EC) No 2073/2005 on microbiological criteria for foods, applicable from 1 January 2006, lays down food safety criteria for relevant foodborne bacteria, their toxins and metabolites, such as Salmonella , Listeria monocytogenes , Enterobacter sakazakii , staphylococcal enterotoxins and histamine in specific foods.

rules.limit: Verification required against FDA regulations and inventories

rules.evidenceQuote: Approved Food and Color Additives and Petitions Under Review; Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) Citations for Color Additives, Food Ingredients and Packaging

rules.limit: Not allowed for use in food (21 CFR 81.10 and/or 81.30). Verification required.

rules.notes: These are labeled as 'DELISTED'.
rules.evidenceQuote: Delisted color additives in FDA regulations (21 CFR 81.10 and/or 81.30) (labeled as "DELISTED").

rules.limit: Must be listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Parts 172, 173, 73, 74) or be GRAS (21 CFR Parts 182, 184) or prior-sanctioned (21 CFR Part 181). Verification required.
rules.sourceTitle: Substances Added to Food (formerly EAFUS) | FDA
rules.notes: The Substances Added to Food inventory includes these types of ingredients. Prohibited or delisted substances are not allowed.
rules.evidenceQuote: Food additives and color additives that are listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Parts 172, 173 and Parts 73, 74 respectively)... Generally Recognized as Safe ("GRAS") substances that are listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Parts 182 and 184). Substances approved for specific uses in foods prior to September 6, 1958, known as prior-sanctioned substances (21 CFR Part 181).

rules.limit: Prohibited from use in food (21 CFR Part 189). Verification required.

rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: The nine major food allergens are milk, eggs, fish, Crustacean shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts, wheat, soybeans, and sesame. Excludes meat, poultry, egg products, alcoholic beverages, raw agricultural commodities, highly refined oils, drugs, cosmetics, and most foods sold at retail or food service establishments that are not pre-packaged with a label.
rules.evidenceQuote: The major food allergens are milk, eggs, fish, Crustacean shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts, wheat, soybeans, and sesame.

rules.sourceTitle: Chemical Contaminants Transparency Tool | FDA
rules.notes: This is a Guidance Level.
rules.evidenceQuote: 3-Monochloropropane-1,2-Diol (3-MCPD) Acid-hydrolyzed protein and Asian-style sauces Guidance Level 1 ppm CPG 500.500

rules.foodCategory: Foods (general), Peanuts and peanut products, Pistachio nuts, Brazil nuts
rules.sourceTitle: Chemical Contaminants Transparency Tool | FDA
rules.notes: This is an Action Level.
rules.evidenceQuote: Aflatoxins, total Foods Action Level 20 ppb CPG 555.400; Aflatoxins, total Peanuts and peanut products Action Level 20 ppb CPG 570.375; Aflatoxins, total Pistachio nuts Action Level 20 ppb CPG 570.500; Aflatoxins, total Brazil nuts Action Level 20 ppb CPG 570.200

rules.limit: Verification required against the Chemical Contaminants Transparency Tool, guidance documents, and regulations (21 CFR parts 109 and 509)
rules.sourceTitle: Chemical Contaminants Transparency Tool | FDA
rules.notes: Includes tolerances, action levels, guidance levels, derived intervention levels, recommended maximum levels, and advisory levels. Users are responsible for staying informed about changes.
rules.evidenceQuote: The FDA has published a variety of contaminant levels for poisonous or deleterious substances in human food in various guidance documents and regulations for industry. For ease of reference, the Chemical Contaminants Transparency Tool provides a consolidated list of these levels. Tolerances and action levels are defined in 21 Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) parts 109 and 509.

rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: Such statements are not required by law and should not be used as a substitute for adhering to current good manufacturing practices. They must be truthful and not misleading.
rules.evidenceQuote: Such statements are not required by law and can be used to address unavoidable “cross-contact,” only if manufacturers have incorporated good manufacturing processes in their facility and have taken every precaution to avoid cross-contact... advisory statements should not be used as a substitute for adhering to current good manufacturing practices and must be truthful and not misleading.

rules.foodCategory: human food (manufactured, processed, packed, or held)
rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: Facilities must put written procedures in place to control allergen cross-contact and ensure accurate allergen labeling, as per the CGMP & PC rule (21 CFR part 117).
rules.evidenceQuote: The FDA’s “Current Good Manufacturing Practice, Hazard Analysis, and Risk-Based Preventive Controls for Human Food” rule (CGMP & PC rule, 21 CFR part 117) establishes requirements applicable to establishments that manufacture, process, pack, or hold human food. The CGMP & PC rule includes requirements for allergen preventive controls to prevent allergen cross-contact in manufacturing and packaging and to prevent undeclared allergens.

rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: Identified as allergenic substances derived from insects.
rules.evidenceQuote: Color additives made from cochineal extract and carmine, which are derived from insects, have been identified as allergenic substances that must be declared on the label of all food and cosmetic products.

rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: The food source of a major food allergen must appear in parentheses following the ingredient name OR immediately after or next to the list of ingredients in a 'contains' statement. Specific type of tree nut, fish species, and Crustacean shellfish species must be declared. Excludes meat, poultry, egg products, alcoholic beverages, raw agricultural commodities, highly refined oils, drugs, cosmetics, and most foods sold at retail or food service establishments that are not pre-packaged with a label.
rules.evidenceQuote: FALCPA requires that foods or ingredients that contain a "major food allergen" be specifically labeled with the name of the allergen source... The allergen's food source must be declared at least once on the food label in one of two ways. The name of the food source of a major food allergen must appear: In parentheses following the name of the ingredient... OR Immediately after or next to the list of ingredients in a “contains” statement.

rules.foodCategory: all products containing FD&C Yellow No. 5
rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: Required to be identified on labels for consumers sensitive to the dye.
rules.evidenceQuote: The FDA requires all products containing FD&C Yellow No. 5 to identify it on their labels so consumers who are sensitive to the dye can avoid it.

rules.limit: Must meet a defined standard for gluten content. Compliance requirements for fermented and hydrolyzed foods also apply.
rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: Helps consumers with celiac disease.
rules.evidenceQuote: On August 2, 2013, the FDA issued a final rule defining “gluten-free” for food labeling, which helps consumers, especially those living with celiac disease, be confident that items labeled “gluten-free” meet a defined standard for gluten content. On August 12, 2020, the FDA issued a final rule to establish compliance requirements for fermented and hydrolyzed foods, or foods that contain fermented or hydrolyzed ingredients, bearing the “gluten-free” claim.

rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: Must be declared on food labels when present at or above the specified concentration due to adverse reactions in sensitive individuals (e.g., asthma).
rules.evidenceQuote: they must be declared on food labels when present in food and the concentration in the food is ≥10 parts per million total sulfites.
